revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:粟嶋神社 (海南市)]]
{{Infobox religious building |name = Awashima Shrine (粟嶋神社) |image = File:Awashima Shrine Entrance.jpg | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|location = {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|en|Wakayama Prefecture|qid=Q131314|lt=Wakayama Prefecture|1=Wakayama Prefecture}}, {{ill|Kainan, Wakayama|en|Kainan, Wakayama|qid=Q841309|lt=Kainan, Wakayama|1=Kainan, Wakayama}}, Shimotsu-chō Kata 1101 |位置 = {{Coord|34|07|08.1|N|135|08|59.1|E|region:JP-30_type:landmark|display=inline,title|qq=}} |deity = {{ill|Sukunahikona-no-Kami|ja|少名毘古那神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunahikona-no-Kami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ|1=Sukunabikona|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q3275687}} | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|type = Former {{ill|Murasha (village shrine)|ja|村社|1=Son-sha|qid=Q134917284}}{{efn|Former shrine rank|lt=Son-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Murasha (village shrine)|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q712534}} |創建 = Said to be in the 2nd year of Emperor Keikō's reign |本殿 = Kasuga-zukuri, copper roofing |別名 = Awashima (Dai) Myōjin |札所等 = |festival = {{ill|October 13|en|October 13|qid=Q2925|lt=October 13|1=October 13}} |神事 = Hinamatsuri (April 3) | 地図 = {{Location map|Japan Wakayama Prefecture|label=Awashima Shrine|float=center|relief=1|width=256|qq=}} |prefecture = {{ill|Kainan|qid=Q841309|1=Kainan, Wakayama}} |coordinates = {{Coord|34.118916666666664|N|135.14975|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }} [[File:Torii of Awashima Shrine.jpg|thumb|right|Torii Gate]] '''Awashima Shrine''' (粟嶋神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|qid=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|Kainan, Wakayama|en|Kainan, Wakayama|qid=Q841309|lt=Kainan, Wakayama|1=Kainan, Wakayama}}, Wakayama Prefecture. It is situated on the eastern mid-slope of a hill along the left bank of the Miyagawa, a tributary of the {{ill|Kamo River (Wakayama)|ja|加茂川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo River (Wakayama)|1=加茂川|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q16279097}}, overlooking {{ill|Shimotsu Port|ja|下津港|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimotsu Port|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 和歌山下津港|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 和歌山下津港|1=Port of Wakayamashimotsu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11417301}}'s inlet. The shrine complex includes '''Awashimayama Ryūsen-ji''' (淡嶋山龍泉寺), a former {{ill|Betto-ji (Shinto-Buddhist temple)|ja|別当寺院|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Betto-ji (Shinto-Buddhist temple)|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 別当寺|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 別当寺|1=Bettō-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11396755}}, which retains elements of {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|en|Shinbutsu-shūgō|qid=Q2279088|lt=Shinbutsu-shūgō|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō}} due to avoiding the Meiji-period {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|en|Shinbutsu bunri|qid=Q1970535|lt=Shinbutsu bunri|1=Shinbutsu bunri}} separation. The shrine was historically classified as a {{ill|Murasha (village shrine)|ja|村社|1=Son-sha|qid=Q134917284}}{{efn|Former shrine rank|lt=Son-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Murasha (village shrine)|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q712534}}. == Enshrined Deities == The shrine enshrines {{ill|Sukunahikona-no-Kami|ja|少名毘古那神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunahikona-no-Kami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ|1=Sukunabikona|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q3275687}}, who is believed to be the same deity worshipped at {{ill|Awashima Shrine (Wakayama)|ja|淡嶋神社|1=Awashima Shrine (Wakayama)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Awashima Shrine (Wakayama)|13=User:Immanuelle/Awashima Shrine (Wakayama)|qq=}} in {{ill|Wakayama (city)|en|Wakayama (city)|qid=Q200747|lt=Wakayama City|1=Wakayama (city)}}, Kada. The shrine is revered for {{ill|Bumper crop|en|Bumper crop|qid=Q4997332|lt=Gokoku hōjō|1=Bumper crop}} (bountiful harvests), maritime safety, and matchmaking. Additionally, Sukunahikona is worshipped as a deity of medicine and exorcism, attracting devotees from as far as {{ill|Kantō region|en|Kantō region|qid=Q132480|lt=Kantō region|1=Kantō region}}, {{ill|Tōhoku region|en|Tōhoku region|qid=Q129465|lt=Tōhoku region|1=Tōhoku region}}, {{ill|Shikoku|en|Shikoku|qid=Q13991|lt=Shikoku|1=Shikoku}}, and {{ill|Kyushu|en|Kyushu|qid=Q13987|lt=Kyushu|1=Kyushu}}, especially for curing women's ailments, similar to Awashima Shrine in Kada.<ref name="白水社">Maruyama Akihiro, "Awashima Shrine" in Nihon no Kamigami (白水社『日本の神々』).</ref> The shrine also shares a strong connection with Awashima Shrine in Kada due to its similar deity, faith, and legends of deity drift ({{ill|Hyōchakushin|ja|漂着神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hyōchakushin|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}). Furthermore, both shrines are located in places called "Kata" (かた) and were part of the ancient {{ill|Ama District (Kii Province)|ja|海部郡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ama District (Kii Province)|1=Kaifu District|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11151382}} during the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|令制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ritsuryō system|1=ryō-based system|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11378314}} period.<ref name="白水社"/> The {{ill|Kiizoku Fudoki|ja|紀伊続風土記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiizoku Fudoki|1=Kii Zoku Fudoki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11604964}} suggests that this shrine may have been founded as a branch shrine ({{ill|Bunrei|en|Bunrei|qid=Q195793|lt=Enshrinement|1=Bunrei}}) of Awashima Shrine in Kada. However, another theory proposes that it originally enshrined the {{ill|Watatsumi Sanjin|ja|綿津見三神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Watatsumi Sanjin|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ワタツミ|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ワタツミ|1=Watatsumi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q1474187}}, deities worshipped by the local maritime tribes ({{ill|Kaifu|en|Kaifu|qid=Q446149|lt=Ama|1=Kaifu}}).<ref>Shimosu Town History (『下津町史』).</ref> In the {{ill|Early modern period|en|Early modern period|qid=Q5308718|lt=Early modern period|1=Early modern period}}, the shrine's honjibutsu (corresponding Buddhist deity) was {{ill|Ākāśagarbha|en|Ākāśagarbha|qid=Q416174|lt=Kokūzō Bosatsu|1=Ākāśagarbha}}, according to the Kiizoku Fudoki. == History == According to shrine records, in the 2nd year of {{ill|Emperor Keikō|en|Emperor Keikō|qid=Q329723|lt=Emperor Keikō|1=Emperor Keikō}}'s reign, Sukunahikona-no-Kami drifted ashore at {{ill|Sumiura|ja|硯浦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumiura|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, where 21 village households enshrined him in a nearby grove. The original shrine site was said to be in the sea, accessible only at low tide.<ref>The Manyōshū poem "潮満たば いかにせむとか方便海（わたつみ）の神が手渡る海部未通女（あまおとめ）ども" (Kokutai-kan No. 1216) is believed to describe this practice. (Prefectural Shrine Records).</ref> During {{ill|Empress Jingū|en|Empress Jingū|qid=Q232803|lt=Empress Jingū|1=Empress Jingū}}'s campaign against the Three Han states, she supposedly went into labor and prayed at the shrine to delay childbirth until her victorious return. A strong storm prevented her envoy from reaching the shrine, forcing them to perform rituals from a distance.<ref>There is a shrine precinct called "Heishi-no-Kuma" (幣使隈), where these offerings were supposedly made.</ref> After her return, she gave birth to {{ill|Hondawake no Mikoto|ja|誉田別尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hondawake no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 応神天皇|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 応神天皇|1=Emperor Ōjin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q317997}} (later {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|en|Emperor Ōjin|qid=Q317997|lt=Emperor Ōjin|1=Emperor Ōjin}}) on her ship and celebrated by carving statues of Sukunahikona-no-Kami and {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主}} with an arrowhead, offering them to the shrine along with battle drums and a Tang-style robe. In the late 13th century ({{ill|Bun'ei|en|Bun'ei|qid=Q1010103|lt=Bunei|1=Bun'ei}} era), the shrine was moved to its current location for better access. The shrine then received extensive lands from {{ill|Ninna-ji|en|Ninna-ji|qid=Q1202871|lt=Ninna-ji|1=Ninna-ji}}, the lord of {{ill|Hamanaka-shō|ja|浜中荘|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hamanaka-shō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 長保寺|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 長保寺|1=Chōhō-ji (Kainan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q2972129}}, allowing it to flourish.<ref>Hamanaka-shō was a shōen (estate) covering the Shimosu area. The Konoe family (近衛家) was the main titleholder.</ref> This led to the spread of the shrine's worship nationwide.<ref>Prefectural Shrine Records.</ref> By the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|qid=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, the shrine had an established national presence, as evidenced by a golden heisoku (御幣) offering from a resident of {{ill|Yonōzu|ja|米水津|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yonōzu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Bungo Province|en|Bungo Province|qid=Q864148|lt=Bungo Province|1=Bungo Province}} (present-day {{ill|Saiki, Ōita|en|Saiki, Ōita|qid=Q847546|lt=Saiki, Ōita|1=Saiki, Ōita}}, Ōita Prefecture) in {{ill|Hōei 4|ja|宝永4年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōei 4|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (1707).<ref>Yonōzu's connection to the shrine dates back to the {{ill|Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|en|Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|qid=Q8140349|lt=Nanboku-chō period|1=Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)}}, when {{ill|Prince Kaneyoshi|en|Prince Kaneyoshi|qid=Q11074321|lt=Prince Kaneyoshi|1=Prince Kaneyoshi}} safely landed there after praying at the shrine during a storm.</ref> In {{ill|Kansei 9|ja|寛政9年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansei 9|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 1797年|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 1797年|1=1797|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q7820}} (1797), worshippers from {{ill|Kiryū, Gunma|en|Kiryū, Gunma|qid=Q634022|lt=Kiryū, Gunma|1=Kiryū, Gunma}}, Gunma, donated a sacred bridge, which is now a Kainan City-designated cultural property. During the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|qid=Q3125096|lt=Meiji period|1=Meiji era}}, the shrine was officially classified as a village shrine and later merged with five smaller shrines.<ref>These included the Hamamiya Shrine (字硯), Benzaiten Shrine (字姫ノ淵), Dōsojin Shrine (字宮川), Ushigami Shrine (字北原), and Ebisu Shrine (字北原). (Shimosu Town History).</ref> It is now part of the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|en|Association of Shinto Shrines|qid=Q908077|lt=Association of Shinto Shrines|1=Association of Shinto Shrines}}. == Festivals == The shrine's main festival is held on '''October 13''', while '''Hinamatsuri''' is observed on '''April 3'''. == Cultural Properties == '''Kainan City Designated Cultural Property''' * '''Stone Sacred Bridge''' (昭和50th year , November 3, 1975) – A taikobashi bridge originally spanning the Miyagawa river, later relocated in 1990.<ref>Kainan City historical marker, 1990.</ref>
== See Also == * {{ill|Awashima-no-Kami|ja|淡島信仰|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Awashima Faith|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 淡島神|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 淡島神}} * {{ill|Kinomiya Faith|ja|キノミヤ信仰|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinomiya Faith|qq=}}
{{translated page|ja|粟嶋神社 (海南市)|version=92776568|comment=Imported full ja history}}
[[zh:粟嶋神社]] [[ja:粟嶋神社 (海南市)]]
